---
schema: verdi.decisionconflict/v1
covers: 6dfc7a8151ca7030bd945f2c00cf4b445ed465cb
findings:
  - { id: judged-dc-overlap-default-and-design-ref, kind: judged, text: "spec/ref-index dc-3 declares Source a CLOSED four-value enum (default/local/remote/both) 'covering every source ComputeIndex reads', where 'both' covers only the local+remote design-ref overlap. But parent spec/workbench-directory dc-4 makes a merged-but-not-yet-gc'd design branch a normal, expected state (gc reclaims lazily on merge/deletion signals; directory reads never delete), and ref-index dc-4 independently enumerates the default branch's own tree. In that normal state the same spec is reachable from BOTH the default branch and a still-present design ref, a source combination the closed enum cannot express — forcing ComputeIndex to emit either two entries for one spec or one entry with an understated source. Two entries would place the same spec simultaneously in a draft group and in accepted-pending-build/active/terminal (which ref-index dc-4 says are populated only from default-branch content), contradicting parent dc-2's rule that 'the status is the distinction' — one spec cannot carry two statuses; a single entry with a picked-winner source silently drops a source, contradicting parent dc-5's 'each entry disclosed by source'. No supersedes/exempts edge is declared for this merged-lingering case, and unlike the closed origin-scope and StatusGroup-for-degraded-entry findings, none of dc-1..dc-4's disclosed scope lines address it. (confidence 0.55)", target_ref: spec/workbench-directory }
sweep_provenance: { adr_corpus_digest: sha256:37517e5f3dc66819f61f5a7bb8ace1921282415f10551d2defa5c3eb0985b570, decisions_scanned: [spec/ref-index#dc-1, spec/ref-index#dc-2, spec/ref-index#dc-3, spec/ref-index#dc-4, spec/workbench-directory#dc-1, spec/workbench-directory#dc-2, spec/workbench-directory#dc-3, spec/workbench-directory#dc-4, spec/workbench-directory#dc-5] }
digest: sha256:3058bf57e8a2a3fe82814bacc2a56cf353c83a223ecde7db3d1901030ca55cb3
integrity: sha256:aa31b1b3cb920248ecfe4c5b3b251d7b27e57a48a57a4b00dec84a17b9431e8b
judge_integrity: { stdin_b64: , raw_result: "{\"findings\":[{\"id\":\"dc-overlap-default-and-design-ref\",\"text\":\"spec/ref-index dc-3 declares Source a CLOSED four-value enum (default/local/remote/both) 'covering every source ComputeIndex reads', where 'both' covers only the local+remote design-ref overlap. But parent spec/workbench-directory dc-4 makes a merged-but-not-yet-gc'd design branch a normal, expected state (gc reclaims lazily on merge/deletion signals; directory reads never delete), and ref-index dc-4 independently enumerates the default branch's own tree. In that normal state the same spec is reachable from BOTH the default branch and a still-present design ref, a source combination the closed enum cannot express — forcing ComputeIndex to emit either two entries for one spec or one entry with an understated source. Two entries would place the same spec simultaneously in a draft group and in accepted-pending-build/active/terminal (which ref-index dc-4 says are populated only from default-branch content), contradicting parent dc-2's rule that 'the status is the distinction' — one spec cannot carry two statuses; a single entry with a picked-winner source silently drops a source, contradicting parent dc-5's 'each entry disclosed by source'. No supersedes/exempts edge is declared for this merged-lingering case, and unlike the closed origin-scope and StatusGroup-for-degraded-entry findings, none of dc-1..dc-4's disclosed scope lines address it.\",\"confidence\":0.55,\"target\":\"spec/workbench-directory\"}]}" }

# Decision-conflict report

## Computed (declared-edge completeness)

(none)

## Judged (undeclared-conflict sweep)

- **judged-dc-overlap-default-and-design-ref** [UNDISPOSITIONED]: spec/ref-index dc-3 declares Source a CLOSED four-value enum (default/local/remote/both) 'covering every source ComputeIndex reads', where 'both' covers only the local+remote design-ref overlap. But parent spec/workbench-directory dc-4 makes a merged-but-not-yet-gc'd design branch a normal, expected state (gc reclaims lazily on merge/deletion signals; directory reads never delete), and ref-index dc-4 independently enumerates the default branch's own tree. In that normal state the same spec is reachable from BOTH the default branch and a still-present design ref, a source combination the closed enum cannot express — forcing ComputeIndex to emit either two entries for one spec or one entry with an understated source. Two entries would place the same spec simultaneously in a draft group and in accepted-pending-build/active/terminal (which ref-index dc-4 says are populated only from default-branch content), contradicting parent dc-2's rule that 'the status is the distinction' — one spec cannot carry two statuses; a single entry with a picked-winner source silently drops a source, contradicting parent dc-5's 'each entry disclosed by source'. No supersedes/exempts edge is declared for this merged-lingering case, and unlike the closed origin-scope and StatusGroup-for-degraded-entry findings, none of dc-1..dc-4's disclosed scope lines address it. (confidence 0.55)
